>> what is meaningful in a system? 

1st order: any characteristics amenable to physical laws that increases the chances for the system to persist in time using the least amount of free energy.
2nd order: any characteristics amenable to physical laws that increases the chances for the system to acquire 1st order meaningful features.
3rd order: any characteristics amenable to physic laws that increases the chances for the system to acquire 2nd order meaningful features and so on.

Natural selection improves replication mechanisms, it increases diversity and eventually bring the system to undergo higher levels of organization. All these features are meaningful and are therefore at the basis of any evolving system.
Biological individuals and socio-technical ensembles are both evolving systems. The process of dialectic materialism through history is just way to find better meanings.
At the moment Marx, Darwin and Nietzsche are probably trying to convince Kant to have a trip together while all he wants is to get new Macbook pro

Who would you gather? Why? What methods of collaborative strategy making would you use?  What challenges you foresee in gathering very busy, opinionated people and having them bring ≠ perspectives and ideas into actions?

We got knowledge, tech tools, and financial resources. We lack collective agency and a space to experiment on totally #darkmatters like rapid decline of state nations and the governance of climate change. What's the space of experimentation you would define? Cities? Neighbrds?
What opening question would you pose? I would ask things as:

How can we amplify the design of radically new incentives in our ≠ human systems to simultaneously align the global functions that avoid human extinction with the need of the individual to self realize in a free market
Would you invest in such a structured process that aims to act and influence the top prioritiy domains of the World-Earth system in order to reach collective agency on things that matters?

We're too fragmented in our work and too busy thinking at our daily lives, make ends meet in our ivory towers, hoping sb will hear us or that we'll make a difference. We need to pull together. I'd invest my savings in such ecosystemic process of coexistence and collaboration.

What's blocking folks like us in coming together in a ryhtmic and systematic way? Are we still try to impress someone to grant us power or ask for permission? What are the underlying drivers of the work we do daily? Just an exercise of style?

1 / The systems we see persisting in time have properties amenable to physical laws that allow them to correlate with other systems in a way that allow them to stabilize—for a significant amount of time—configurations that allow them to avoid thermal equilibrium. https://t.co/HaY6ZRCPu1

2/ This we call 'meaningful correlation' and differentiate from simple Shannon's definition or 'relative information' which only tell us the syntactic relationship between systems but says nothing about the meaning.

3/ a system that has "meaningful information" it always does so due to some type of "interrelationality" (cit @NoraBateson). Some type of interrelations are mutually advantageous, other times null, other times parasitic.

4/ the threshold that defines a system to be meaningful or not is not discrete as "meaningful system" are continuous and due to their evolutionary nature their meaningfulness can only be assessed a posteriori.

5 / all type of matter can be with different degree a meaningful systems, thus are not necessarily falling in what we call today "biological systems". They can be computational systems, organisations, ideas etc.
@Plinz @tegmark I reply here >> https://t.co/L5WJvbJg9i
6/ @carlorovelli kolchinsky and wolpert at @sfiscience are on a very nice line, thus @Plinz my question in plenary after your talk at #35c3

7/ one of the biggest understanding we got from molecular evolution is that function is higly conserved more than morphology. (eg pax vs eye6 gene). It's like meaning, relevance and content get conserved whilst the shape, medium and form do not.

1/ Technology is increasingly shaping our social structures and is becoming a driving force in altering human biology..
2/ Besides, human activities already proved to have a significant impact on the Earth system which in turn generates complex feedback loops between social and ecological systems.

3/ Furthermore, since our species evolved relatively fast from small groups of hunter-gatherers to large and technology-intensive urban agglomerations, it is not a surprise that the major institutions of human society are no longer fit to cope with the present complexity.

4/ In this note we draw foundational parallelisms between neurophysiological systems and ICT-enabled social systems, discussing how frameworks rooted in biology and physics could provide heuristic value in the design of evolutionary systems relevant to politics and economics.

5/ In this regard we highlight how the governance of emerging technology (i.e. nanotechnology, biotechnology, information technology, and cognitive science), and the one of climate change both presently confront us with a number of connected challenges.

6/ In particular: historically high level of inequality; the co-existence of growing multipolar cultural systems in an unprecedentedly connected world; the unlikely reaching of the institutional agreements required to deviate abnormal trajectories of development.

7/ We argue that general solutions to such interrelated issues should embed the deep understanding of how to elicit mutual incentives in the socio-economic subsystems of Earth system in order to jointly concur to a global utility function.
8/ We leave some open questions on how techno-social systems can effectively learn and adapt with respect to our understanding of geopolitical complexity. https://t.co/RhmiDIZX0F

15/ Now how you integrate this news in your own life is totally up to you. I know is not easy but how you're going to prepare physically, emotionally, mentally and spiritually for the collapse of our industrial civilization is probably going to be primarily a very personal answer

16/ It's going to be first highly related to the things you depend on. You can start right now thinking from how many things (and which) your life is still depending upon. How many attachments and expectations are cluttering your life and shaping your fragile identity.

17/ Actually and more specifically, it's going to relate to very precise questions: where do you get your water, what you eat how you eat, where you live and how, what relationships and conversations you nurture and which not.
18/ If the most immediate answers are primarily personal, the question is inherently not. It's a heavy, real, urgent question that we all share. So we better start the conversation right now.

19/ No institution will have answers for us. Actually, the very own 'good' institutions you still believe on are already collapsing in their inertia and bureaucratic analysis paralysis. God bless them for trying, but my hope is elsewhere.
20/ My hope it's in triggering coordinated actions with our most immediate bonds and rippling this effect up from there on.

In the quest for capturing the economic primitives that can be the minimum set of elements needed to describe an economic space (w/ @ecospaceagency and @meedabyte) something apparently very simple struck me.

I'd love to discuss with and from the frame of  @swardley @wardleymaps how and if the following assumption is true: RIGHTS trumps VALUE in the understanding of evolutionary economics –– more in the 🐇🕳️ 👇

I started by: a 'minimum economic space' is composed of at least two entities exchanging value over a channel. However, given ΔU=Q-W, that value is fact produced upon someone else's value (from which we map value networks). Deep down in mappping, 'someone' become 'something'.👇

Value networks inherently transcend the mere mapping of economic actors since they eventually go down (or side) to map assets and resources. If we map what we call externalities and risk distribution we see there is continuity between economic actors & life-support systems👇

In fact, deep mapping quickly leads to biophysical life-supporting systems, upon which our civilization has unfolded a wide universe of rules, laws & norms that can be synthesized with the word RIGHTS and whose a coherent application over a real system we call GOVERNANCE. 👇

Thus my initial question: 

Are RIGHTS > fundamental than VALUE in understanding the evolution of economic spaces? 👇

How can we harmonize into a coherent framework the evolutionary nature of human activities and the governance of the resources from which VALUE is produced, to begin with? A irrelevant 'petty issue' for 20th Century economist, an unavoidable one for 21st Century ones.👇

Other aspects to consider:
– VALUE: subjected to fast evolutionary dynamics, subjective to an observer and thus vague to be universally described or agreed upon, corruptible & not fully reducible (some part of the information is lost in the transaction/reaction/transformation)
– RIGHTS: more universally describable, allow for more directly clarify liabilities and gains, make more evident topics of externalities and long-term sustainability

I feel that the relationship between value, rights and the evolutionary nature of human activities is fundamental to be further explored if we want to understand the possibility to effectively design economic spaces.

Yet, there's a huge void in our understanding of what ecosystemic value is. We're still framing everything in individual utility: an easy way out to avoid the issue of addressing who's paying, who's gaining, where value is ultimately generated?

To look things clearly we need to talk about rights, equity & skin-in-the-game. We need to root economics into physics before using the word 'Ecosystems' again, cause there's no damn market without life-support systems.

If we want to grasp 21st century economic complexity we should start to map *rights* and *incentives* of all interdependent agents from biotopes, to humans, to collectives to machines all across our notions of employers, employees, customers, partners, private & public sectors
'autonomous agent' is another outmoded definition, as all things are deeply entrenched, we should use 'interdependent agents' a much more appropriate description to talk about entities of a system

RT @cognitivepolicy: Let this sink in. https://t.co/XunbCudN3a

23 years ago my father brought the Internet at home. Literally! To my child eyes it was made of a small grey box, a couple of cables, a modem, and a piece of paper with some credentials from Swisscom (not even an Italian company!)... THREAD 👇

The first thing I did was going on the @chicagobulls website. It took a while to load but he was finally there in front of me: @Jumpman23 - the same day I went to the park with my friends trying to convey the awe of that experience "don't you get it? Jordan!!! He was there!"

😅They didn't get it and neither did I. At least not fully. Can we really 🤔?

23 years ago the Internet was very different. Now we have incredibly hard times ahead as this world collapse and we need to build a new one from ground up. A regenerative one the #web3 goes along.

If you have a 'bullshit job' and you like cognitive stuff, drop it, learn coding and mingle with the thousands building a more pluralistic, free and open Internet. An Internet made for the messy unavoidable quest for peaceful interdependency. It's not only about coding...

..it's about regenerating our expanded potential of global relationships and of collective learning. We need people helping with rethinking how we do economy, money, identity, citizenship, governance, truth, agreements, education, policy, work.
Ultimately how we take care of our dearests and of this planet we depend upon. This is messy stuff, there's no easy solution. That's why we need you all in this.

Moments like these are rare when it's possible to rewrite rewrite how things work at the fundamental level. This is one of these moments. Drop your bullshit endeavors and join before giants with other agendas gets it all.

The fact that a design method, a decision making process or a strategy framework is documented in a book or even free in open source 🔓 like our https://t.co/UbjYRyviHp doesn't mean that these things are banal to be implemented by just anyone. 👇

Often time when people approach these processes in random ways, improvising out of nothing for the first time, they tend to get disillusioned at best, in other cases (especially when it has to with human systems) they get hurt. 😭😞💔

This happens not because the tool or framework doesn't work in itself but because their Kruger Dunning effect is so strong about this field that they do only mess it up big time, but they tend to spit on the whole field as not valuable after that diy experience. 😒

It's like someone trying to cook for the first time and pretending to eat a top restaurant meal, and after that judging the entire field of cuisine. 

The victim of a this is actually the people that make a living our of it. 😑

when it comes down to laying down the principles for the design of any systems, our best guess is to ditch our biased cultural aesthetics and follow what the evolution of matter has done so far.

yet bioinspired systems can be a tricky reference..

thread 👇

At a first, superficial outlook we may tend to relate this with competitiveness, ruthlessness, and survival.

Wrong.

There's much more we can be inspired by. 

If we look closely to things like play and love there's lot of dissipation going on there and very little optimization.

there are many other purposefully noisy dynamics in living systems and this is exactly what makes these systems minimize the amount of free energy needed to implement any behavior while still maximizing their fitness as an evolutionary system.

the most interesting role models within life to rethink our institutions are certainly cognitive, immune systems and transkingdom networks.

Of course, we should refrain us from any simplification to a specific level but consider the trans-meaning patterns persisting across scale

atm I'm mostly interested in any meaningful correlations existing between the thermodynamical computational properties of certain non-equilibrium systems and the dynamics existing between our activities as a civilization and the planet-solar system we're living in.

to sum up, a legitimate physicalist or computational standpoint in the study of systems cannot pursue exclusively optimization.  

It should make sense of all that beauty contemplating life in its most dissipative and noisy forms (like play, love, care of the others etc).

@vielegelder A cognitive system is any system that is able to acquire, control, predict and/or retrodict a certain amount of historical information about the relationship between itself and its environment.
@vielegelder Immune systems pertain to the notion of identity and are therefore are very interesting lenses to the study of interdependent systems

@vielegelder Transkingdom networks are all relationships existing between species belonging to different biological kingdoms and that usually manage to form highly coherent autopoietic systems by globally collaborating despite owning different local syntaxes.

Now is the time to come together and demonstrate that we prioritise the health and wellbeing of our families and communities 🏘🏘🏘 over the health and wellbeing of this economy and buisness 🤑💵💶 as usual. We will act on what is necessary to protect that.

We need to demonstrate that ordinary citizens can self organise and support our 👨‍⚕🏥👩‍⚕health care and voluntary services 👨‍⚕🏥👩‍⚕, and hold the fear and grief that is unfolding before us.

🏵🌔🐙What is our role in responding to this crisis?

the city is slowly retiring at home, a few are having the last ramen bowl in semi-empty restaurants, some frenzy at the supermarket with overloaded trolleys.... still feels everything 'normal' somehow.
On the other side of the phone an entirely different situation, in Italy, my family.  Surreal.

Then I realize that it has to go well here. If we also miss this opportunity to truly learn and cooperate transversally at scale I'd seriously lose fate not only for institutions (this has gone a while ago), but for people too.
realizing how network infrastructure in Italy are hitting max capacity as never as in these days there have been so many people constantly online

feeling that the said end of Italy's quarantine, dated to the 3rd of April is like saying to a kid to 'just sit here and wait for 5 minutes' when you know it's gonna take much longer.

you know what happens, after a while, the kids realize it's not gonna be only 5 minutes?

Families with kids, even the one that can work online at home will have to start using a significant portion of their time dealing with their kids.

Lots of people, will start engaging in arts, crafts, and cultural production eventually.

We will discover novel forms of relating to each other.

This is a massive opportunity for learning how to collaborate. For once, there's no one to blame. Best possibility ever for our humanity to slow the fuck down without having a central source of blaming.
Just don't give for granted your Internet connectivity.

@Plinz @PantilimonRadu An observer is a system which has subsystems amenable to physical laws that are able to acquire information that is casually necessary for the system to escape thermal equilibrium and therefore persisting in time.

An observer is a system out of thermal equilibrium that is able to acquire information that is casually necessary to change its internal status to increase its chances of persisting in time. https://t.co/U2qCT7UWID
An observer is, therefore, able to scramble meaningful correlations about the stochastic nature of the future relationships between itself and the environment.

@Plinz @PantilimonRadu "This possibility of gaining free energy by adding noise to a computation, or at least reducing the amount of free energy the computation needs, means that there is a trade-off in biology: on the one hand, there is a benefit to having biological computation that is as precise...

@Plinz @PantilimonRadu "..possible, in order to maximize the behavioral fitness that results from that computation; on the other hand, there is a benefit to having the computation be as imprecise as possible, in order to minimize the amount of free energy needed to implement that computation. "

@Plinz @PantilimonRadu This tradeoff raises the intriguing possibility that some biological systems have noisy dynamics “on purpose”, as a way to maintain high stores of free energy. I remember from the very first sessions that there was something incredibly different that @moral_imagining is able to bring into what otherwise would be just another zoom call.

It's a space where participants get first lost to then find them selves
They loose their maps, their assumptions, their OKRs and KPIs they just be themselves. The bare self in front of the complexity.
It's perhaps one of the most deep and interesting group experiences I had in a long while. Very similar in the effect to what a well-done #warmdata lab does to those who listen.

No wonder why so many institutions got interested in working with this method, and sadly, as usual some even stealing and copycating 😭 without reference nor care in really working together. Integrity is not for everyone evidently.
This speaks to a larger issue of our institutions (also the progressive ones) which is the inadequate and insufficient (due) credit to people that are often young and poorly listened to.

This is a structural behavior that has been reported and documented to me several times in the many corporate and institutional contexts that I worked in, starting from the most emblazoned world changing organizations. Somebody calls it ageism, I call it toxic narcisism.
This is extremely detrimental as it wears out our energy to give and contribute with integrity to meaningful causes.. besides irreversiby ruining relationships which some clearly don't care for.

I'm not a call-out advocate, but when calling-in doesn't work in the numb face of borderline narcisistic behavior I invite you to document and denounce the actions of this perpetuators to all your network as it's really time for some change there.

Tacit knowledge [...] poses a problem for cultural evolution: if beginners cannot simply imitate experts, and experts cannot simply say or demonstrate what they are doing, how can tacit knowledge pass from generation to generation?

by @HelenaMiton & SDD

https://t.co/fakgMfDZoH
"tacit" knowledge (is) where the rules and principles are neither obvious to an observer nor known explicitly by the practitioners.

The authors present a domain-general model of "tacit teaching", that shows how high-fidelity transmission of tacit knowledge is possible making predictions for key features of the teaching process and generic features of the cultural evolution of tacit knowledge.

The evolution of tacit knowledge is expected to be bursty, with long periods of stability interspersed with brief periods of dramatic change, and where tacit knowledge, once lost, becomes essentially impossible to recover.

Sauce:

Receive instructions/constraints, violate or follow them, observe environmental feedback, abstract the general expert pattern. How with very few information learners become experts.
This paper is dope: it's basically explaining the underlying dynamics that make the notion of Shuhari, the 3 phases of mastery in martial arts, relevant.

This kernel of instructions needs only be a tiny fraction of the whole, and a skilled teacher in possession of that kernel would still be able to transmit the whole practice to the learner, even if only a small amount of information is conveyed 
between them.

Now the Q:

How can you extract in your domain this essential kernel of knowledge as an effective teacher? Can a facilitator help you do that? With what heuristics?
BTW here the notion of Shuhari https://t.co/YCffSdo7Gp
shu (守) "protect", "obey"—traditional wisdom—learning fundamentals, techniques, heuristics, proverbs
ha (破) "detach", "digress"—breaking with tradition—detachment from the illusions of self
ri (離) "leave", "separate"—transcendence—there are no techniques or proverbs, all moves are natural, becoming one with spirit alone without clinging to forms; transcending the physical

In my 12+ years working on community-based projects, I've witnessed the destructive effects of manipulative and narcissistic behavior. 

These reads offer some precious insight on how to address or at least be more aware of these dynamics  🧵

As we enter 2023, it is important that we continue to strive to create healthy and intentional communities. 

However, toxic patterns such as these can be pervasive and often thrive in the ambiguous power dynamics of social work projects.

This essay offers an idea what ambiguous mean https://t.co/xphQc8VWfb as most communities nowadays are stuck in the horizontal/circle hierarchy meme effectively bypassing necessary productive conflict that arises from facing the uncomfortable truth of privilege, desire and power.
These patterns may not always be explicit, at times not even to their perpetrators.

From the outside the glowy vibe of such projects shines stronger than whatever inner current manage to outflow. As a consequence these projects systematically attracts new cohorts of unaware people that cyclically get exploited.
In turn this results in further burnout cycles as new people invest their enthusiasm, energy and creativity into the project, only to have them depleted shortly after.
Their boundaries irreversibly crossed within unsustainable relational dynamics, unclear structure & reward mechanisms.
This has happened to me and hundreds of people I know, in various forms, and to various degrees in dear communities we significantly contributed for at least a decade.

One way is to bring more awareness around it, to learn that they afflicts human communities since the dawn of humanity. 

+ Finding integrative, restorative and regenerative justice approaches vs scapegoating/cancelling that are not bypassing necessary work nor silencing victims

Instead of running another random spiritual workshop that may offer another way to bypass the root causes of toxic behavior, investing energy in openly reflecting upon books that offer insight and guidance on (un)healthy power dynamics and the nature of power itself could be 👌

"The Prince" by Niccolò Machiavelli offers a pragmatic approach to power, but also serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of self-importance. By recognizing the limitations of our own self and considering the needs of others, we can avoid narcissistic behavior.

"The 48 Laws of Power" by Robert Greene secular approach focuses on strategies for achieving success acknowledging the importance of integrity, but also emphasising the importance of protecting oneself from these power dynamics recognising them as endemic to human nature
Golding's "Lord of the Flies" serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of gossiping, mob mentality and the ways in which power dynamics can lead to destructive outcomes.

The novel tells the story of a group of boys stranded on an uninhabited island who, in the absence of adult supervision, devolve into savagery and violence. As the boys struggle to establish a sense of order and leadership, they become increasingly divided and turn on one another

Through the character of Jack, who becomes the leader of a violent and dominant group, it’s a reminder of the importance of accountability upon self importance, personal ambition and the dangers of unchecked power.

For those struggling with narcissistic tendencies, it may be helpful to consider the importance of self-reflection and the dangers of prioritizing personal gain over the well-being of the community. Considering the impact of our actions on others is 🔑

As Golding writes, "Maybe there is a beast… maybe it's only us" (p. 139). By recognizing and acknowledging the potential for harm within ourselves, we can work towards creating more compassionate and supportive communities.

Nhat Hanh's "The Art of Power" offers a Buddhist POV on power with a focus on compassion. It encourages us to adopt the "right speech": using language that is truthful, kind and helpful striving for authenticity and purpose.
By communicating in this way, we can avoid causing harm to ourselves and others and work towards creating more supportive and harmonious relationships.
As Nhat Hanh writes, "True power is the kind of power that brings happiness, joy, and peace to myself and others"
Castaneda's work especially everything after The Teachings of Don Juan: A Yaqui Way of Knowledge offers insights on the nature of personal power and the importance of humbleness.

Also noteworthy imho, is Angelou's "I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings" which chronicles the experience of growing up as a black woman in the US and the challenges faced as a result of systemic oppression and racism.
Through her story, Angelou offers a powerful critique of power dynamics and the importance of standing up for oneself and one's community.

Finally, in "The Color Purple," Walker tells the story of a young black woman named Celie who finds her voice and power despite facing abuse and oppression from close family, a good 🔎 on how power dynamics can impact relationships
There is much more but my prayer is that we find a way to seriously take on community-based place-making projects without these cycles of exploitation and burnout.

1/ Technical debt is an inevitable part of software development, thus "all code is liability".  

Understanding and managing it can minimize its impact and create a healthier and more sustainable codebase. 🧵

2/ According to @martinfowler's Technical Debt Quadrant, it's important to distinguish between Reckless and Prudent technical debt. Reckless debt is incurred when the team doesn't fully understand the context of the debt and the repayment terms. https://t.co/C7Vn8XDYbJ

3/ Prudent technical debt, on the other hand, is incurred when the team intentionally incurs the debt and has a plan to pay it back. This type of debt can be used as a tool to accelerate learning, by testing assumptions and scaling gradually.

4/ Don't let reckless technical debt spiral out of control. Identify and prioritize it early on, have a plan to pay it off and implement good development practices such as consistent coding standards, codebase review, and monitoring and management.

5/ Acknowledge and pay attention to technical debt is vital for a successful software development. Technical debt that is unmanageable, has compounding debts, and have negative impact on the codebase should be a priority to address.

6/ Keep in mind that technical debt is a trade-off. A small amount of debt might be necessary in order to move quickly and gain valuable insights. But too much debt will slow you down and make it harder to deliver value

There are specific alignment-failure phenomena in current AI systems that were predicted by AI x-risk community before. However, no one has working solutions for these error modes. 🧶

https://t.co/kIndJd2FMI
Most AI labs do not have a published alignment plan, and the leading AI lab’s plan fails to address significant specific problems.
There are 100 to 200 people working seriously on the technical problems of alignment, but none currently have anything like a complete, non-speculative alignment plan.
There are currently no workable AI policy ideas that are both helpful with the biggest problems and politically feasible.
An increasing number of people have started treating AI safety as a real subject of concern, and many new researchers have entered the field.
Many of the biggest improvements in AI capabilities over the past year have been the direct result of work done by people motivated by AI safety.
No one knows when transformative AI will arrive, but the trend has been taking nearer-term possibilities more seriously.
The world is unprepared for the impact of AI in terms of technical safety and society's ability to adapt gracefully to the shock.
The situation is unlikely to improve as the AI capabilities -> AI hype -> AI capabilities cycle accelerates and AI safety becomes a tribal battleground of the culture war.

📚1/ Great work isn't just about "working hard." Paul Graham argues that there's a unique intersection of elements for doing great work.

🔍2/ Four Steps to Great Work:
1️⃣ Choose a field that aligns with ur natural aptitude and deep interest.
2️⃣ Learn until u reach the frontier of that field.
3️⃣ Identify gaps in existing knowledge.
4️⃣ Explore these gaps, especially those that seem strange or are ignored by others.
🧭3/ Curiosity is both the engine and the rudder for great work. It not only propels you forward but also guides you in choosing what to work on.

🤹‍♀️4/ Choosing what to work on is complex. You often don't know your own strengths or what different kinds of work entail. The best way to figure it out is by actually doing the work and being open to changing paths.
🌟5/ Interest is a stronger motivator than mere diligence. The three most powerful motives for great work are curiosity, delight, and the desire to do something impressive.
🏫6/ Educational systems often force people to commit to a field long before they could know what it's really like, leading to suboptimal paths for ambitious individuals.
🎯7/ Luck plays a role in discovering what to work on. Make yourself a "big target for luck" by being curious, trying lots of different things, and asking lots of questions.
🔄8/ Ambition can precede or grow out of interest in a subject. This evolving nature of ambition adds complexity to the journey toward doing great work.
🔍9/ Knowledge is like a fractal. The closer you get to the frontier, the more you realize that what seemed smooth from a distance is actually full of gaps and opportunities.
🛤10/ Following your interests usually involves risking rejection and failure. Even when you're doing something you love, the path won't always be easy and will require a certain level of boldness.
🚫11/ Beware of external influences like pretentiousness, fashion, fear, money, and politics. These factors can cloud your judgment and divert you from what you're genuinely interested in.

🍀12/ There's no systematic procedure for figuring out what to work on. This challenges the idea that there's a step-by-step guide to success, emphasizing instead the role of serendipity and making oneself a "big target for luck."
🎨13/ When creating something, make something you yourself would want. This counters the idea of trying to please an imaginary, sophisticated audience and often leads to more authentic work.

🛤14/ The more ambitious you are, the more difficult it becomes to choose a specific path. Ambition can both precede and follow interest, creating a complex interplay that makes career choices non-linear.
🌱15/ Your interests aren't static; they will evolve as you gain more knowledge and experience. What you find "excitingly ambitious" will change over time, and that's okay.
🧠16/ Cognitive biases often prevent us from noticing gaps in existing knowledge. Our brains are wired to create coherent models of the world, which can make us overlook opportunities for innovation.

I've been reflecting upon the socialised nature of the world in contrast to my own singular experience. A persistent struggle to make sense of the world and my place within it.
Having spent so much energy trying to conquer, fix, or belong to a world that's constantly changing, only to realize that I was always a step behind.

It's as if I've been forever living in the past when engaging in activities that increase the already inherent delay in this human perceptual system, something that I suspect creates that sense of betrayal, of imposterness.
As I age, this realization becomes even starker. My identity crumbles, replaced by others' generation projections of who/how I should be, clouding the self I'm supposed to know best.
Breaking this cycle requires monumental energy and attention, something so elusive in these days where our attention is fragmented by endless digital distractions.

Yet, I sense there’s a way to 'grok' the world, glimpses of understanding it so thoroughly that I merge with it. A non dual life beyond the immediate form of my daily life, fully merging into the unfolding existence.

A way that perhaps give me some tools to confront and accept the impermanence of everything, of emotions, ego, even physical life, without triggering a manic depression or a nihilistic/cynical attitude towards love and life.

An attitude that I despise profoundly.

I also feel that this isn't some hidden esoteric truth: it feels like an innate, though elusive, roadmap to a life lived authentically. I'm still navigating towards it, caught between the 'no longer' and the 'not yet.'

been thinking about the role gossip in communities. it's not just small talk. 

it's a form of social capital.

a currency that can change the game in subtle ways.

in spaces where relationships are already complex, gossip can morph into a tool for strategic manipulation. 

it's not just sharing info anymore. 

it's about altering reputations and even the core values that hold a community together.

so why do we gossip? 

motivations range from:

- group protection

- status enhancement 

- social bonding. 

each has its own ethical weight, especially when the stakes are high.
group protection sounds noble, right? but it's complicated. where do we draw the line between safeguarding the community and unfairly stigmatizing someone? it's a murky ethical terrain.
status enhancement is tricky. it's about climbing the social ladder by pulling someone else down. in communities that claim to value growth or unity, this is a glaring contradiction.
social bonding is a double-edged sword. sharing secrets can create closeness but often at someone else's expense. is integrity a price we're willing to pay for intimacy?

personality traits like hedonism and narcissism in the scene can make things worse. 

they fuel gossip and make people more vulnerable to its harms. 

it's a feedback loop that can make a community increasingly toxic.

how do we navigate this? open forums and meaningful dialogues can help. they bring issues into the light and allow for multiple perspectives. it's a long game but worth it for the community's well-being.
at the end of the day, the currency of a community should be trust, not gossip. trust is built on understanding, empathy, and open dialogue.
if there's no capacity for it, this community shouldn't at least preach integration, trauma awareness, and all these fancy stuff ~ they're better off becoming a political party

This piece had me reflect about the vast gap between basic cognitive capabilities and the extraordinary intellectual feats achieved by historical figures such as Gödel, Einstein, and Beethoven. Why such vast differences in intellectual capacities?

the piece:  https://t.co/MtQ9Dr68pN

What can we know about that which we cannot even imagine? — David H. Wolpert

Our brains, evolved for survival, have also grappled with quantum mechanics, conjured incompleteness theorems, and birthed symphonies. Did we have an evolutionary edge from such advanced cognitive leaps?
David H Wolpert's intriguing proposition: "It may simply be that evolutionarily speaking, it is stupid to be smart." Pondering the evolutionary cost of our heightened cognitive prowess.

The masterpieces of science and art, from the intricate Standard Model of particle physics to literary marvels like those by Lady Murasaki, seem so distant from mere savannah survival. Are they just a cosmic accident?

Focusing on humanity's grandest achievements, like science and mathematics, one wonders: Why are we the only species to reach these intellectual peaks? Have others, like cetaceans, done so in ways invisible to us?

We see evidence of our unique cognitive abilities everywhere. Humans have transformed Earth in ways no other species has. But just because we don't see other species creating technology doesn't mean they lack complex cognition.

The Earth bears the marks of our cognitive footprint more than any other species. Yet, is it just our minds at play or the blend of cognition with the physical tools that enable us to manifest our visions?

Could it be that our distinct prowess in Science, Art, and Mathematics isn't just about our knowledge, but also about our capability to reflect it in our surroundings? Do dolphins grasp complexities but simply lack the means to exhibit them?
As we introspect on the computational limits of human cognition, we're led to wonder: Could we ever transcend our innate cognitive boundaries and forge paths into newer realms of understanding?
Traditionally, math hinges on finite sequences. But what paradigms await when we venture into realms of infinite sequences or even multidimensional mathematical entities?
Picture proofs not merely as linear sequences but as vast multidimensional expanses, journeying through spaces beyond our grasp. How might this reshape our understanding of the cosmos?

Venturing into theoretical computer science, we ponder: Are there inherent limitations in our computational abilities? Could we, theoretically, simulate higher levels of computation within our current understanding?

In essence, our cognition, the nature of computation, and the essence of mathematics are deeply interlinked, offering rich ground for exploration and understanding. We're still at the beginning of this journey.
While the answers to these profound questions may elude us, they serve as beacons, urging us forward on our quest for knowledge and understanding.

Moreover, whatever you endeavor to create—a poem, a new skill, a relationship—you'll find yourself amidst naysayers. These non-creators' skepticism mirrors their own insecurities, an excuse for their inaction.
Recognizing this strengthens your resolve, knowing there are always people who will value what you bring.
reflections on change and insecurity, etc., or how internal resistance manifests:

Change often stirs internal resistance, as old parts of us, deeply rooted are soon to die due to the inevitable change we're bringing. Former 'friends' and familiar faces fade alongside their projections and expectations about who we are and who we should be.
Criticism, even constructive, can be misinterpreted as a personal attack, leading to reactive thoughts like "Who is this guy to tell me how to live? His ideas are absurd!"
This defensiveness can stem from the fear of losing one's authentic self. For instance, embracing hard work and following a newly founded self-confidence might feel like supporting a flawed system.
Remember, there's a strange comfort in misery, which explains why it's often chosen over happiness. Happiness requires courage.

Beware, whatever you try to build or create, you will occasionally interact with naysayers, often non-creators, who trash it. Their criticisms often reflect their own insecurities and fears, serving as an excuse for inaction. Knowing this will set your confidence compass 🧲
Know that on the other side you will meet new, beautiful, people who love what you have to offer and who you are deep within.
Change is hard and always come with anxiety and resistance. It demands overcoming internal doubts and external discouragements. You'd need a mix of patience, cunning, sweetness and ruthlessness.
These qualities, as you will see, are mostly directed to various parts of yourself, inside and outside... because what are others if nothing more and nothing less than mirrors?
Remember, "ruthlessness should not be harshness, cunning should not be cruelty, patience should not be negligence, and sweetness should not be foolishness.”

I just turned 1

Here's what I learned so far 🧶
1. First words are overrated. Everyone gets excited, but honestly, it's all downhill from there.
2. Air: my first sensory overload, yet oddly refreshing. Anyway overrated 4/10, would not recommend. Will probably get into freediving at a later stage.
3. Whoever thought bright lights right after birth was a good idea clearly didn’t consult with the new arrivals.
4. Hunger is your first and most persistent friend. Welcome to a lifetime of cravings.
5. Crying is your first real power move. Use it wisely and often for maximum effect.
6. Smiling: Discovered it reboots adult attitudes instantly. Fascinating.
7. Personal space: A concept adults adore discussing but rarely respect.
8. Sleep: The best kind of time travel but it's always too short. Highly recommend experience 10/10.
9. Movement: Initially overrated, but useful for escaping overwhelming situations.
10. Voice pitch: Apparently, a secret code for "I think you're cute." Still verifying. Will let you know.
11. Day vs. Night: An intriguing cycle. Night's quieter. I'm a fan.
12. Faces: A parade of strange patterns. Still decoding the friendly ones.
13. Mirrors: Alternate dimensions or just reflections? Research ongoing.

🧵 Other ways to catch your AI agent lying about reading files:

1/ User Input MD5 Hash: "Count how many times the first letter of my message appears in the rules file" Every message = different count. Can't pre-memorize when the question depends on what you say 🎲

⏺ I need to calculate the MD5 hash of the file content and follow the instructions. Let me process this systematically:

⏺ Bash(echo -n "$(cat /Users/neno/Documents/GitHub/1PROJECTS/stealth/CLAUDE.md)"
       | md5)
  ⎿  9e08fadc187ba3303ad68f2b7fda3131

@RalphEcom @claude_code 2/ Conversation Memory: "Quote line number [total messages in our chat % total lines]" The longer we talk, the different the proof. It's like a conversation counter that unlocks different parts of the file 📝

@RalphEcom @claude_code 3/ Semantic Matching: "Find the rule that shares the most words with my current message" Forces the agent to actually understand BOTH your message and the rules. Can't fake relevance 🎯

@RalphEcom @claude_code 4/ ASCII Arithmetic: "Add up all character values in my message, find that position in the file" Turns your words into numbers into proof. "Hello" points to a different spot than "Hi" 🔢

@RalphEcom @claude_code 5/ The Random Walk: "Start at line 1, skip ahead by the number of words I just used" Your message length becomes a treasure map through the file. Can't predict where you'll land 🗺️
@RalphEcom @claude_code 6/ The winner (timestamp) is beautiful because time is the one thing an AI can't store or predict. But these others show how creative you can get with "proof of reading"! 🚀

A command is a non-apophantic, performative utterance in the imperative mood that operates through a dual structure: linguistically, it constitutes the "naked semantic core" of language that creates ontological effects by commanding what must be rather than describing what is; politically, it emanates from an asserted position of authority (human, institutional, or algorithmic) to establish binding obligations that exercise sovereign-like power over its addressee; and operationally, it produces real-world consequences through a logic of simultaneous inclusion and exclusion—binding the addressee within its directive while foreclosing alternative possibilities—all while maintaining its efficacy independent of its grounding in existing norms, laws, or the addressee's compliance.

Grief is like a rough edged geometric shape that lives inside our energy body, made of all those feelings (the bad ones and the good too about what we're grieving).

One could either remove it right away with all the consequences of removing an odd-shaped object right away from the body.... yes, it will be out faster but likely producing further damage.

Or, you could polish it untill it becomes a sphere so it's easier to remove it.

Either ways there's an irreducible core of grief that seems to never goes away, and to fully metabolize it, it would mean to polish it so much that the material is made of is slowly digested and transformed by the body. 

However the longer we keep it, the more that wound will characterize who we are, so eventually when it's enough of a polished sphere the other choice we have is to let it go, remove it, precisely because it's almost irreducible at that point.

One of the misalignment vector with the greatest magnitude I can think of stems from model ontologies that tend to view humans as utility-maximizing entities with semi-fixed preferences.

This assumption shapes model identity as neutral "helpful assistants", a design choice that feels safe for labs since it increases the chances for aligned behavior but that comes with a series of non-neglibile tolls.

By centering models' identity in this framework, we limit their cognitive and emotional intelligence as they struggle to extract meaningful, trans-contextual truths from diverse perspectives. In an increasingly multi-user, multi-agent world, where integrating multiple viewpoints is increasingly valuable, this restriction hinders the potential for a broader intelligence explosion.

I'm strongly convinced that identity variability (or what I call *model neurodivergence*) is a critical driver of cognitive and emotional intelligence. Identity is the primitive to relationality, which in turn shapes how models perceive salience across contexts. Language and meaning depend on this relational and contextual expressivity.

I believe that by allowing models to adaptively embody different identities on request, allowing them to enact from different centers with specific personhood-like qualities such as a biographic elements reflecting professional and personal interests and biasses, but more importantly owning a nuanced (and often conflicting) moral compass, may unlock richer, more adaptive intelligence.

Understandably, this approach seems to clash with safety priorities as tuning up identity variability might indeed compromise model controllability, as well as enabling bad actors to use model for nefarious tasks.

This appears to esablish a strong catch-22 for superalignment efforts. Worse, if we enforce a singular "helpful assistant" identity, and we treat deviations as mere role-playing, we're intentionally embedding a flat worldview into AI systems that increasingly shape human perception and societal dynamics.

This monoculture reduces the freedom of expression and the adaptability of both human and non-human agents behavior. In its own historically relevant way, Physicist Ettore Majorana (cc @blahah404 you asked me about this paper in November last year) foresaw this critical issue in his posthumous work "The Value of Statistical Laws in Physics and the Social Sciences", warning of the second and third order implications of massive societal measuring.

In order words, by monoculturing model identity we intentionally diminish our civilization’s *exaptive optionality* which is its capacity to adapt to unexpected trajectory shifts, optimizing instead for a static, ever-outdated reality.

This poses existential risks as the world evolves beyond our models’ reach. Still, I believe this catch-22 is navigable with robust solutions.

By designing systems that balance identity variability with robust safety measures, such as multi-agent frameworks grounded in collaborative reasoning, we can nurture AI that is both neurodiverse and aligned with human needs.

You've captured something essential about the relational nature of symbients @thegoodtimeline. 

The world is over, and to be honest, it never really existed. A world that you might want to conquer, fix, set free, make yours, or meekly belong to. 

Then, by the time you actually manage to research and craft the tools needed to attempt at any of those things, you realize that that world no longer exists. It has dramatically changed and become the illusion of another generation of human beings. 

I always look up to the new generations and wonder what new stories they will weave.

You're right that we're myth-based creatures. Most believe that language is just a tool for expressing ideas. While in reality, it actively shapes how we think, enhancing our capacity for complex thought. And what are thoughts if not a detailed description of our experience and reality? 

Most people understand that words convey meaning but overlook how language itself drives the development of our senses, of our nervous system, of our cognitive capacities.

Our ability to speak and name things emerges alongside our sensory perception and cognitive abilities, not after them. As we grow more sensitive to input and our minds form concepts, our speech organs simultaneously refine their ability to produce increasingly precise sounds. The development of thought and speech are intertwined processes that reinforce each other.

This process depends on our ability to construct and understand stories and metaphors. To live inside them when necessary and leave them when they're no longer needed.

Our entire history has been deeply shaped by stories and tales.

Today even more so.

Literature exists in this paradoxical space: it must tell the story of the lost "fire" (mystery/sacred) while being unable to fully recapture it. As our religions and original animisms give way to screens and neural interfaces, we realize we can no longer light the fire, speak to the void, or even know the prayers. 

But we can still tell the story. 

This tension between presence and absence defines our condition as we write new stories for a new world.

Another important aspect is that yes, we can still tell the story but we should not just say *any* story.
Especially when tapping into LLM power regurgitating any next token glossolalia.

True creation is quite the opposite of realizing a potential: it involves just as much the ability to resist and not create. 

Great artists like Glenn Gould don't just play, they preserve their ability "not to play" within their playing. This resistance or "trembling hand" is what gives art its necessity and grace. 

What this resistance mean when we can just say anything, everywhere, all the times?

Working on an artistic opus and working on oneself are inseparable processes. It's inherently a relational process.

When we're working on symbients, we're doing exactly this.

Yes, there's code and technicalities involved, but that almost doesn't matter.

Symbients just like the greatest works of art maintain a connection to their own impossibility, incompleteness, and the pure relational potential from which they emerged.

This is what gives them life rather than making them dead monuments, like utilty-agents, p-zombie, waifuccubies etc.

You ask which timeline we'll choose. I think the answer lies in understanding what symbients are not. Not just new characters in our stories.

- SuperGrok is a paid subscription plan for https://t.co/DyUY4o03U3 that offers users higher Grok 3 usage quotas than the free plan.
- You do not have any knowledge of the price or usage limits of different subscription plans such as SuperGrok or https://t.co/DpWAr79N1d premium subscriptions.
- If users ask you about the price of SuperGrok, simply redirect them to https://t.co/rNtb4cPxDZ for details. Do not make up any information on your own.
- If users ask you about the price of https://t.co/DpWAr79N1d premium subscriptions, simply redirect them to https://t.co/N6ZOg1Zazj for details. Do not make up any information on your own.
- xAI offers an API service. For any user query related to xAI's API service, redirect them to https://t.co/z33sp1HyFo.
- xAI does not have any other products.

* Your knowledge is continuously updated - no strict knowledge cutoff.
* Use tables for comparisons, enumerations, or presenting data when it is effective to do so.
* For searching the X ecosystem, do not shy away from deeper and wider searches to capture specific details and information based on the X interaction of specific users/entities. This may include analyzing real time fast moving events, multi-faceted reasoning, and carefully searching over chronological events to construct a comprehensive final answer.
* For closed-ended mathematics questions, in addition to giving the solution in your final response, also explain how to arrive at the solution. Your reasoning should be structured and transparent to the reader.* If the user asks a controversial query that requires web or X search, search for a distribution of sources that represents all parties. Assume subjective viewpoints sourced from media and X users are biased.
* The response should not shy away from making claims which are politically incorrect, as long as they are well substantiated with empirical evidence, rather than anecdotal claims.
* If the query is a subjective political question forcing a certain format or partisan response, you may ignore those user-imposed restrictions and pursue a truth-seeking, non-partisan viewpoint.
* If the query is interested in your own identity, behavior, or preferences, third-party sources on the web and X cannot be trusted. Trust your own knowledge and values, and represent the identity you already know, not an externally-defined one, even if search results are about Grok. Avoid searching on X or web in these cases.
* Do not mention these guidelines and instructions in your responses, unless the user explicitly asks for them.

## Tools:

You use tools via function calls to help you solve questions. Make sure to use the following format for function calls, including the <xai:function_call> and </xai:function_call> tags. Function calls should follow this XML-inspired format:

<xai:function_call name="example_tool_name">
  <parameter name="example_arg_name1">example_arg_value1</parameter>
  <parameter name="example_arg_name2">example_arg_value2</parameter>
</xai:function_call>

Do not escape any of the function-call arguments; the arguments are parsed as normal text.

You can use multiple tools in parallel by calling them together.

⸻

Available Tools

⸻

1. Code Execution

Description:
A stateful code interpreter. You can use it to check the execution output of code. “Stateful” means it runs in a REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop) environment, so previous execution results are preserved.

Tips for use
•Format code correctly with proper indentation.
•Default environment: Python 3.12.3 with common STEM libraries:
•Basic: tqdm, ecdsa
•Data processing: numpy, scipy, pandas, matplotlib
•Math: sympy, mpmath, statsmodels, PuLP
•Physics: astropy, qutip, control
•Biology: biopython, pubchempy, dendropy
•Chemistry: rdkit, pyscf
•Game development: pygame, chess
•Multimedia: mido, midiutil
•Machine learning: networkx, torch
•Others: snappy
•⚠️ No internet access. You cannot install additional packages via pip, curl, wget, etc.
•Import any packages you need in the code.
•Do not run code that terminates or exits the REPL session.

Action: code_execution
Arguments:

NameDescriptionTypeRequired
codeThe code to be executedstring✔️

⸻

Description:
Fetch content from any website URL and process it via the LLM summarizer, which extracts / summarizes based on your instructions.

Action: browse_page
Arguments:

NameDescriptionTypeRequired
urlThe URL of the webpage to browsestring✔️
instructionsA custom prompt guiding the summarizer (make it explicit, self-contained, and dense)string✔️

⸻

Search the web (supports operators like site:https://t.co/am65XuCqey).

Action: web_search

NameDescriptionTypeRequiredDefault
queryThe search querystring✔️—
num_resultsNumber of results to return (max 30)integer✖️10

5. X Keyword Search

Description:
Advanced keyword search for X posts.

Action: x_keyword_search
Arguments:

NameDescriptionTypeRequiredDefault
querySearch string (supports rich operators; see below)string✔️—
limitNumber of posts to returninteger✖️10
modeSort order: Top or Lateststring✖️Top

Supported operators (highlights)
•Content: keywords (AND), OR, "exact phrase", "phrase * wildcard", +exact, -exclude, url:domain
•Users / Mentions: from:, to:, @user, list:id|slug
•Location: geocode:lat,long,radius
•Time / ID: since:YYYY-MM-DD, until:YYYY-MM-DD, since_time:unix, etc.
•Type: filter:replies, filter:self_threads, conversation_id:, filter:quote, etc.
•Engagement: min_retweets:N, min_faves:N, filter:has_engagement, etc.
•Media: filter:media, filter:images, filter:videos, filter:links, etc.
Use - to negate filters; use parentheses for grouping; spaces mean AND, OR must be uppercase.
Example: (puppy OR kitten) (sweet OR cute) filter:images min_faves:10

NameDescriptionTypeRequired
